steps.title: Find the Key Signal
steps.content: The decisive clue is Caron’s claim: they argue that the apparent tentacles in Chinese and U.S. fossils are comb rows of ctenophores. Weakening this claim requires undermining their conclusion that these structures are definitively ctenophore-specific.

steps.title: Match the Meaning
steps.content:
Choice B — The fossils found in China and the United States are so poorly preserved that though they cannot be conclusively identified as jellyfish, they cannot be conclusively identified as ctenophores either. shows that the fossils are indistinguishable due to poor preservation, making Caron’s identification of comb rows implausible.

The other choices either ignore the central identification issue, address irrelevant locations, or highlight preservation without challenging the claim.

That matches Choice B — The fossils found in China and the United States are so poorly preserved that though they cannot be conclusively identified as jellyfish, they cannot be conclusively identified as ctenophores either..
